summary: McKenzie recommends an SEO guide for bloggers and its companion piece revealing the marketing strategy behind it. He argues that content can be both genuinely useful and strategically designed for viral distribution without being cynical, citing Rails as another example of quality work that was also professionally marketed. He also promotes an upcoming blogging book to which he's contributing a chapter, and closes with a plug for his Christmas bingo cards product.
key_points: Genuinely useful content and strategic viral marketing are not mutually exclusive — you can design something to spread while still providing real value
Understanding how linkbait works (via the followup article) is as valuable as the SEO advice itself
Rails serves as a case study that professional marketing and viral design don't compromise quality
money_quotes: There is no reason you shouldn't promote things which are useful to your audience for your mutual benefit.
Just because you're professionally marketed and designed to go viral doesn't mean you have to suck.
key_insight: Content marketing works best when the content is genuinely useful — designing something to go viral and designing something valuable are complementary goals, not conflicting ones.
